Triggers.value: Being in a natural environment similar to where it happened

Maladaptive Strategies.value: I dwell on what other people have done to me
keep mind occupied all the time

Threat.value: I study people's faces and body language to detect if they are a threat
I have difficulty falling asleep because I need to stay alert
Suddenly feeling or acting as if the stressful experience were actually happening again

Memory.value: Sometimes I can recall what happened but feel completely numb, as if it happened to a stranger
When I remember the event I feel intense shame
My memories of the frightening event are mostly pictures or images.

issues.item: Verbal Access: I can talk about what happened very easily.
issues.explanation: This conflicts with the rest of the memory profile, especially 'Emotionless Recall' and 'Shame on Recall,' which indicate distress and altered emotional access when recalling the event. Easy verbal recall can coexist with numbness, but here it weakens the coherence of the memory node and the trauma-linked pattern implied by the active Triggers↔Memory edges.

issues.item: Safe Zone Only: I only feel safe in a very limited number of places
issues.explanation: This is not clearly incoherent by itself, but it is only weakly aligned with the formulation because Threat has no active links from Triggers in this profile, while the dominant active structure is Triggers↔Memory and Threat→Memory. The item is plausible but does not strongly fit the specified edge pattern and appears more global than the rest of the threat profile.

vignette:
Mark, a 43-year-old European roofer, was referred after persistent distress following a terrorist attack that occurred while he was outdoors in a natural area on a work break, with open ground around him and nowhere to retreat. He describes the moment of peak danger as hearing the sudden impact of objects being thrown nearby, then feeling his own heart start hammering while people scattered. He froze rather than moving decisively, and afterward kept replaying how exposed he had felt. Since then, he has remained on edge in places that resemble that setting, especially when he is working near trees, fields, or other open green spaces.

When Mark speaks about the event, he does not tell it as a smooth story. He breaks it into isolated pictures: the angle of the ground, the motion of objects flying, the look of people’s bodies as they ran, and the sensation of his own pounding heart. At times he says he can remember what happened yet feel completely numb, “like it happened to someone else.” At other times the images hit him with such force that he seems pulled back into the scene, briefly acting as if it is happening again.

His central stuck point is close to: “What happened proved I am powerless, and it was humiliating that I could not protect myself.” When he remembers the attack, he reports intense shame, and that shame sits tightly alongside the images.

Mark stays alert for danger in other people’s faces and body language, studying strangers on the street and at work as he moves around building sites. He also has difficulty falling asleep because he feels he must stay watchful. At night, even in bed beside his wife, he keeps scanning the room and checking whether he can relax.

The most active cycle in Mark’s difficulties is that the triggers pull up the memory, and the memory in turn brings the triggers back. A stretch of woodland near a job site, or seeing someone throw an object during a heated argument, can bring the images and pounding heart sharply to mind. Once that happens, he works harder to keep his mind occupied, and he avoids wearing clothes that resemble what he had on that day. He dwells on what other people have done to him, especially when he is alone after work, and his wife notices he is mentally elsewhere during evenings at home. On roofing jobs, his need to stay alert makes it hard for him to settle on breaks, and it leaves him exhausted before the day is done.
